questions.question: This planet is the closest to the Sun.
This element, with atomic number 79, has the chemical symbol Au.
This physicist developed the general theory of relativity.
This ancient wonder of the world is the only one still standing.
This document, signed in 1215, limited the power of the English monarch.
This empire, lasting from 27 BC to 476 AD, was centered in modern-day Italy.
This fictional wizard attended Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry.
This 1994 film follows the life journey of a man sitting on a bench telling his story.
This singer released the albums 'Purple Rain' and 'Sign o' the Times'.
This is the longest river in Africa.
This country has the most natural lakes in the world.
This strait separates Europe from Asia at Istanbul.
This author wrote 'Romeo and Juliet' and 'Hamlet'.
This dystopian novel features a character named Winston Smith.
This Colombian author wrote 'One Hundred Years of Solitude'.
This sport uses a shuttlecock.
This country has won the most FIFA World Cup titles.
This boxer was known as 'The Greatest' and changed his name from Cassius Clay.
This Austrian composer wrote 'Eine kleine Nachtmusik' and 'The Magic Flute'.
This band released the album 'Abbey Road' in 1969.
This genre of music originated in New Orleans in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
This Italian dish consists of wide, flat pasta layered with sauce, cheese, and meat.
This spice, derived from the Crocus sativus flower, is the most expensive spice by weight.
This Japanese rice wine is brewed using a fermentation process involving koji mold.
This company created the iPhone.
This programming language, created by Guido van Rossum, is named after a British comedy group.
This protocol, the foundation of data communication on the World Wide Web, stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol.
This 1997 James Cameron film features a doomed ocean liner and a famous 'I'm the king of the world!' scene.
This director is known for films like 'Pulp Fiction', 'Kill Bill', and 'Inglourious Basterds'.
This 1982 sci-fi film, directed by Ridley Scott, is set in a dystopian 2019 Los Angeles and follows a detective hunting replicants.
This president, the 16th, is famous for the Emancipation Proclamation and his top hat.
This president purchased the Louisiana Territory from France in 1803, doubling the size of the nation.
This president served the shortest term in office, dying of pneumonia just 31 days after inauguration.
This is the largest land animal on Earth.
This marine mammal is the largest animal to have ever lived on Earth.
This tiny bird, native to Cuba, is the smallest bird species in the world at just over 2 inches long.
This animated show follows a yellow-skinned family in the town of Springfield.
This HBO fantasy series, based on George R.R. Martin's novels, features the Iron Throne.
This British sitcom, set during World War II, follows the misadventures of Captain Mainwaring's Home Guard platoon.
This is the smallest continent by land area.
This African country is home to Mount Kilimanjaro, the tallest peak on the continent.
This landlocked Central Asian country is the largest landlocked country in the world by area.
This force keeps us on the ground and causes objects to fall.
This organ in the human body is responsible for filtering blood and producing urine.
This subatomic particle, theorized by Peter Higgs, was confirmed by CERN in 2012 and is associated with giving particles mass.
This 'King of Pop' performed the moonwalk and released the best-selling album of all time.
This rock band, fronted by Freddie Mercury, is known for 'Bohemian Rhapsody' and 'We Will Rock You'.
This composer went almost completely deaf yet continued to compose, including his famous 9th Symphony.
This Greek god is the ruler of the sea and carries a trident.
This Norse god wields the hammer Mjolnir and is the god of thunder.
In Greek myth, this king was condemned to stand in a pool of water beneath fruit trees, with both receding whenever he reached for them.
This sport is played on a diamond-shaped field with bases.
This tennis player holds the record for the most Grand Slam men's singles titles.
This country has won the most Olympic gold medals in the Summer Games all-time.
This wall, built to keep invaders out of China, stretches over 13,000 miles.
This 1945 conference divided post-WWII Germany among the Allied powers and was attended by Truman, Stalin, and Churchill.
This ancient trade network connected China to the Mediterranean, facilitating the exchange of silk, spices, and ideas.
This is the tallest type of tree in the world, found in California.
This natural phenomenon, caused by charged particles from the Sun hitting Earth's atmosphere, creates colorful lights in polar skies.
This deep-ocean trench in the western Pacific is the deepest known point on Earth's surface, reaching nearly 36,000 feet.
This mustachioed plumber, created by Nintendo, rescues Princess Peach from Bowser.
This sandbox game, created by Markus 'Notch' Persson, lets players mine and craft in a blocky, procedurally generated world.
This 2015 action RPG, the third installment in a Polish studio's fantasy series, features a monster hunter named Geralt of Rivia.
This children's author wrote 'The Cat in the Hat' and 'Green Eggs and Ham'.
This novel by F. Scott Fitzgerald is set in the Jazz Age and features a mysterious millionaire on Long Island.
This Irish modernist author wrote 'Ulysses', a novel following a single day in Dublin through an experimental stream-of-consciousness technique, and 'Finnegans Wake'.
This planet in our solar system is known for its iconic rings.
This astronaut was the first human to walk on the Moon in 1969.
This dwarf planet, discovered in 2005 and larger than Pluto, is the most massive known dwarf planet in the solar system.
This popular breakfast food, often served in a stack with syrup, is made from batter cooked on a griddle.
This fermented Korean side dish is made primarily from salted and seasoned napa cabbage.
This French culinary term refers to preparing all ingredients and having them measured and ready before cooking begins.
This Leonardo da Vinci painting depicts a woman with a famously enigmatic smile.
This Dutch painter, who cut off part of his own ear, created 'The Starry Night'.
This art movement, pioneered by Braque and Picasso around 1907, depicted subjects from multiple viewpoints using geometric forms.
This language is the most spoken native language in the world by number of speakers.
This ancient writing system used by the Egyptians features symbols representing sounds and concepts.
This constructed language, created by L.L. Zamenhof in 1887, was designed to be a universal second language.
This 1994 animated Disney film features a lion cub named Simba.
This director created 'Jaws', 'Schindler's List', and 'Jurassic Park'.
This 1957 courtroom drama, directed by Sidney Lumet, takes place almost entirely in a single jury room.
This social media platform, known for short videos, was originally called Musical.ly.
This author created the fictional universe containing hobbits, elves, and the One Ring.
This Japanese filmmaker directed 'Spirited Away', which won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature in 2003.
This mathematical constant, approximately 3.14159, represents the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter.
This number system, used in computing, uses only the digits 0 and 1.
This sequence of numbers, where each term is the sum of the two preceding ones, begins 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13...
This is the largest state in the United States by area.
This narrow, 50-mile-long strait between the Upper and Lower Peninsulas of Michigan is spanned by the Mackinac Bridge, one of the longest suspension bridges in the Western Hemisphere.
This U.S. state capital, the highest in elevation at over 5,000 feet, shares its name with a fictional character from 'The Simpsons'.
This general and statesman, the first president of the United States, led the Continental Army to victory in the American Revolutionary War.
This 1969 NASA mission, crewed by Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins, landed the first humans on the Moon.
This French military leader was exiled to the island of Elba in 1814 but escaped to rule France again before his final defeat at Waterloo.
This long-running medical drama, set at a hospital in Seattle, follows the career of Dr. Meredith Grey.
This Netflix sci-fi horror series is set in a small Indiana town and begins with the mysterious disappearance of a boy named Will Byers.
This critically acclaimed AMC drama follows high school chemistry teacher Walter White as he turns to manufacturing and selling methamphetamine.
This city on the Seine River is the capital of France and home to the Eiffel Tower.
This South American country has the continent's largest population and contains the majority of the Amazon rainforest.
This Southeast Asian archipelago nation is the world's largest by number of islands, with over 17,000 spread across the equator.
This element makes up about 21% of Earth's atmosphere and is essential for the respiration of most living organisms.
This gas, released by burning fossil fuels and absorbed by plants during photosynthesis, is the primary greenhouse gas driving climate change.
This massive particle accelerator, operated by CERN beneath the border of France and Switzerland, was used to confirm the existence of the Higgs boson.
This team sport, played on an ice rink, involves players using sticks to shoot a puck into the opposing team's goal.
This Philadelphia 76ers guard, nicknamed 'The Answer', was famous for his devastating crossover dribble and won the NBA MVP award in 2001.
This British Formula 1 driver has won a record seven World Drivers' Championship titles.
This soft Italian cheese, available fresh or aged, is the classic topping for a Margherita pizza.
This creamy Mexican dip, made primarily from mashed avocado, is commonly served with tortilla chips.
This Italian cured meat, made from salt-cured and seasoned pork belly, is a key ingredient in pasta dishes like carbonara.
This 1993 Steven Spielberg film features a billionaire's dinosaur-filled theme park that descends into chaos.
This actress played the lead role of Katniss Everdeen in 'The Hunger Games' film series.
This 1968 Stanley Kubrick sci-fi epic features the AI computer HAL 9000 and a mission to Jupiter.
This British singer-songwriter, known for her powerful voice, released the album '21' and the hit 'Rolling in the Deep'.
This rapper, born Shawn Carter, is one of the best-selling music artists of all time and is married to Beyoncé.
This jazz trumpeter and bandleader recorded the landmark album 'Kind of Blue' in 1959 and is celebrated for pioneering both cool jazz and modal jazz.
This black-and-white bear, native to China, eats almost exclusively bamboo.
This large spotted cat of sub-Saharan Africa is the fastest land animal on Earth, capable of reaching speeds over 70 mph.
This venomous, egg-laying Australian mammal has a duck-like bill, a beaver-like tail, and can detect electric fields with its bill.
This singer, known as 'Queen Bey', released the groundbreaking visual album 'Lemonade' in 2016 and is one of the best-selling artists of all time.
This South Korean boy band, composed of seven members including RM, Jin, Suga, J-Hope, Jimin, V, and Jungkook, became the first Korean act to top the U.S. Billboard Hot 100.
This manga and anime series, created by Masashi Kishimoto, follows a determined young ninja who dreams of becoming the leader of his village.
This Charles Dickens novel, set during the French Revolution, opens with the famous line 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times.'
This J.D. Salinger novel follows the teenage narrator Holden Caulfield as he wanders New York City after being expelled from prep school.
This 19th-century Russian author explored morality and psychology in novels such as 'Crime and Punishment' and 'The Brothers Karamazov'.
This company, founded by Larry Page and Sergey Brin in 1998, operates the world's most widely used internet search engine.
This encrypted messaging app, used by over two billion people, was acquired by Facebook in 2014 for approximately $19 billion.
This open-source operating system kernel, created by Linus Torvalds in 1991, powers everything from Android phones to most of the world's web servers.
This Italian Renaissance artist painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel and sculpted the famous marble statue of David.
This Spanish surrealist painter, known for his flamboyant personality and waxed mustache, created 'The Persistence of Memory' featuring melting clocks.
This Norwegian artist depicted a figure with an agonized expression against a swirling orange sky in his famous 1893 painting.
This white, winged horse of Greek mythology sprang from the blood of Medusa after she was slain by Perseus.
This Athenian hero sailed to Crete and killed the half-man, half-bull creature living in King Minos's labyrinth.
This Norse shapeshifter and trickster, born of two giants but adopted into Asgard, is the god of mischief and father of monsters like Fenrir and the Midgard Serpent.
This president, who served four terms from 1933 to 1945, led the nation through the Great Depression and most of World War II.
This 5th president issued an 1823 doctrine warning European nations against further colonization or interference in the Americas.
This 27th president is the only person in American history to have served as both U.S. President and Chief Justice of the Supreme Court.
This Nintendo action-adventure franchise features a green-clad hero named Link on a quest to rescue Princess Zelda.
This massively popular battle royale game, released by Epic Games in 2017, is known for its shrinking storm, cartoonish style, and building mechanics.
This 2013 PlayStation exclusive follows a hardened smuggler named Joel as he escorts a teenage girl named Ellie across a post-pandemic America.
This is the name of the star at the center of our solar system and the closest star to Earth.
This NASA spacecraft, launched in 1977, is the farthest human-made object from Earth and became the first to enter interstellar space.
This powerful astronomical event occurs when a massive star exhausts its fuel and collapses, triggering a catastrophic explosion that can briefly outshine an entire galaxy.
This boot-shaped European country is home to Rome, Venice, and Milan, and is surrounded by the Mediterranean Sea on three sides.
This country is the largest in the world by land area, spanning 11 time zones across Eastern Europe and northern Asia.
This South American capital city sits on the Pacific coast and is home to over ten million people, making it one of the most populous cities in the Americas.
This pop icon, known as the 'Material Girl', dominated the 1980s with albums like 'Like a Virgin' and 'True Blue'.
This Compton-born rapper's 2012 album 'good kid, m.A.A.d city' is widely regarded as one of the greatest rap albums ever made.
This Irish rock band, fronted by the singer known as Bono, released the anthemic albums 'The Joshua Tree' and 'Achtung Baby'.
This annual championship game, played at the end of the NFL season, is the most-watched television event in the United States.
This Milwaukee Bucks forward, nicknamed 'The Greek Freak', won back-to-back NBA MVP awards in 2019 and 2020.
This American golfer, nicknamed 'The Golden Bear', holds the all-time record for major championship wins with 18 titles.
This colorful arc of light appears in the sky when sunlight passes through water droplets in the air, typically after rain.
This giant cactus, native to the Sonoran Desert of Arizona and Mexico, can grow up to 40 feet tall and live for more than 150 years.
This scientific theory, introduced by Charles Darwin in 1859, explains how species change over successive generations through natural selection.
This 1977 George Lucas space opera, which opens with the crawl 'A long time ago in a galaxy far, far away...', launched one of the most successful film franchises in history.
This 1999 sci-fi film starring Keanu Reeves depicts a dystopian world in which humans are unknowingly living inside a computer simulation.
This 1974 Francis Ford Coppola sequel interweaves the story of Michael Corleone's rise to power with flashbacks to his father Vito's early life as an immigrant.
This unit of measurement, equal to the distance light travels in a vacuum in exactly one second — about 186,000 miles — is the universal speed limit of the universe.
This type of blood vessel carries oxygenated blood away from the heart and distributes it throughout the body's tissues.
This principle in quantum mechanics, formulated by a German physicist in 1927, states that the more precisely you know a particle's position, the less precisely you can know its momentum.
This four-year conflict, fought between 1861 and 1865, pitted the Union states of the North against the Confederate states of the South.
This German dictator rose to power in 1933, led the Nazi Party, and was responsible for the Holocaust and starting World War II.
This 1789 event, in which Parisian citizens stormed a royal prison fortress, is widely regarded as the symbolic beginning of the French Revolution.
This Harper Lee novel, set in the American South, follows lawyer Atticus Finch as he defends a Black man falsely accused of a crime.
This British author created the fictional detective who solves mysteries from Baker Street alongside his partner Dr. Watson.
This ancient Greek epic poem, attributed to Homer, chronicles the events of the Trojan War, centering on the wrath of the hero Achilles.
This hot brewed beverage, made from roasted and ground beans, is one of the most widely consumed drinks in the world.
This classic Italian dish is made by slowly adding warm broth to rice while stirring, resulting in a creamy, starchy texture.
This cold Spanish soup, popular in summer, is blended from raw tomatoes, cucumber, peppers, garlic, olive oil, and vinegar.
This streaming service, which began as a DVD-by-mail rental company, is now the world's largest subscription video-on-demand platform.
This actress played the beloved character Rachel Green on the long-running sitcom 'Friends'.
This Canadian rapper, who first gained fame as an actor on the teen drama 'Degrassi', became one of the best-selling music artists of all time.
This long-running singing competition, hosted for many seasons by Ryan Seacrest, launched the careers of Kelly Clarkson and Carrie Underwood.
This beloved NBC sitcom, set in a New York City apartment building, features the characters Jerry, George, Elaine, and Kramer.
This critically acclaimed HBO crime drama, set primarily in Baltimore, is considered one of the greatest TV series ever made and examines the drug trade, police work, schools, and the media.
This eight-legged arachnid spins silk webs to capture insects for food.
This large African bird is both the tallest and heaviest bird on Earth and also holds the record as the fastest running bird.
This highly intelligent sea creature has three hearts, blue blood, eight arms, and can instantly change the color and texture of its skin.
This is the number of sides on a hexagon.
This mathematical concept, represented by the symbol ∞, describes a quantity larger than any finite number.
This type of number cannot be expressed as a simple fraction and has a decimal expansion that never terminates or repeats, such as √2 or π.
This American pop artist is famous for his silk-screen prints of Campbell's Soup cans and colorful portraits of Marilyn Monroe and Elvis Presley.
This American abstract expressionist, known for his 'drip painting' technique, created large canvases by pouring and splattering paint across them.
This 15th-century Flemish painter, a pioneer of oil painting technique, is known for the Ghent Altarpiece and his extraordinary attention to detail.
This narrow strait connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Mediterranean Sea and separates southern Spain from northern Morocco.
This mountain range forms the natural border between France and Spain, separating the Iberian Peninsula from the rest of Europe.
This Southeast Asian archipelago nation, composed of over 7,600 islands, is the only predominantly Christian country in Asia.
In tennis, this word describes a score of zero for a player.
This American swimmer won 8 gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, setting the record for most gold medals won by any athlete at a single Olympic Games.
This prestigious annual road cycling race, held primarily in France each July, is the most famous event in professional cycling.
This social media platform, founded by Mark Zuckerberg at Harvard in 2004, became the world's largest social network.
This wireless networking technology allows devices to connect to the internet over short distances without a physical cable.
This computing model refers to storing, managing, and processing data on remote internet-connected servers rather than on a local machine.
This Greek demigod, son of Zeus and a mortal woman, was tasked with performing twelve seemingly impossible labors as punishment.
This creature of Greek mythology, born of Queen Pasiphae and a sacred bull, had the head of a bull and the body of a man and lived in the labyrinth of Crete.
This multi-headed dog of Greek mythology stands guard at the entrance to the underworld, preventing the dead from leaving and the living from entering.
This country music legend, known for songs like 'Jolene' and 'I Will Always Love You', also owns the Dollywood theme park in Tennessee.
This Seattle grunge band, fronted by Kurt Cobain, released the landmark album 'Nevermind' in 1991, bringing alternative rock into the mainstream.
This jazz vocalist, nicknamed 'Lady Day', is considered one of the greatest jazz singers of all time and recorded the haunting protest song 'Strange Fruit' in 1939.
This 2016 augmented reality mobile game sent millions of people outdoors to catch virtual creatures using their smartphones.
This 2011 open-world RPG from Bethesda, set in a snowy Nordic province filled with dragons, features the iconic battle shout 'FUS RO DAH'.
This 2017 Nintendo open-world game featuring the hero Link was praised for revolutionizing the action-adventure genre with its physics-based puzzles and climbing mechanics.

questions.question: This president was in office when the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks occurred and subsequently launched the War on Terror.
This 35th president, the youngest ever elected to the office, was assassinated in Dallas, Texas in November 1963.
This 26th president, known as 'The Rough Rider', established the U.S. national park system and was the youngest person ever to assume the presidency.
This form of precipitation consists of ice crystals that form unique six-sided structures as they fall from clouds.
This sudden release of energy in the Earth's crust causes the ground to shake and can trigger tsunamis, landslides, and widespread destruction.
This ecological process describes how a community of plants and animals gradually develops and changes over time in a habitat, eventually reaching a stable state called a climax community.
This is the name of the spiral galaxy that contains our solar system.
This planet, the fourth from the Sun, is called the Red Planet due to iron oxide on its surface and is the focus of many future crewed mission plans.
This icy celestial body, composed of dust and frozen gases, develops a bright glowing tail when it passes close to the Sun.
This punctuation mark, placed at the end of a sentence, signals that the writer is asking a direct question.
This ancient language of the Roman Empire gave rise to Spanish, French, Italian, Portuguese, and Romanian, and is still used in Catholic Church ceremonies.
This literary device assigns human characteristics to non-human subjects or abstract concepts, as in the phrase 'the wind whispered through the trees'.
This 2010 Christopher Nolan thriller follows a team of specialists who enter people's dreams to plant an idea in a target's subconscious.
This 2003 Peter Jackson film, the third in his epic fantasy trilogy, won the Academy Award for Best Picture along with 10 other Oscars.

valid: the lord of the rings the return of the king
lord of the rings return of the king

questions.display: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King

questions.question: This 1927 Fritz Lang silent sci-fi film depicts a dystopian future city divided between an elite ruling class living above and an oppressed working class laboring below.
This genre of music, which originated in the Bronx in the 1970s, combines DJing, rapping, breakdancing, and graffiti art.
This rapper, born Marshall Mathers in Detroit, is one of the best-selling music artists of all time and is known for his rapid-fire delivery and his alter ego 'Slim Shady'.
This Houston rapper released the critically acclaimed 2018 album named after a beloved defunct amusement park in his hometown, featuring hits like 'Sicko Mode'.
This title was given to the rulers of ancient Egypt, who were considered both gods and kings.
This young Egyptian king, who died at approximately age 18, became world-famous when archaeologist Howard Carter discovered his nearly intact tomb in 1922.
This ancient Egyptian goddess, sister and wife of Osiris and mother of Horus, was one of the most widely worshipped deities in the ancient world and is often depicted with large, outstretched wings.
This Marvel superhero, a billionaire inventor who built a powered suit of armor, is portrayed by Robert Downey Jr. in the MCU.
This 2018 Marvel blockbuster features the purple-skinned villain Thanos wiping out half of all life in the universe with a snap of his fingers.
This Marvel character, a former neurosurgeon who becomes the Sorcerer Supreme and master of the mystic arts, is played by Benedict Cumberbatch in the MCU.
This process, performed by plants, uses sunlight to convert water and carbon dioxide into glucose and oxygen.
This double-helix shaped molecule, found in the nucleus of every living cell, carries the genetic blueprint for all life on Earth.
This cellular organelle, often called the 'powerhouse of the cell', produces energy in the form of ATP through the process of cellular respiration.
In American football, this scoring play — worth six points — occurs when a player carries or catches the ball across the opposing team's goal line.
This quarterback, who played for the New England Patriots and Tampa Bay Buccaneers, won seven Super Bowl championships — the most of any player in NFL history.

This Hall of Fame wide receiver, who played most of his career with the San Francisco 49ers, holds the all-time record for career receptions with 1,549 and is widely considered the greatest at his position.
This NBC sitcom, which ran from 1994 to 2004, followed six friends navigating relationships, careers, and life in New York City from their coffee-shop booth at Central Perk.
This HBO crime drama, which premiered in 1999, follows New Jersey mob boss Tony Soprano as he juggles his criminal organization with the demands of his actual family.
This NBC political drama, which premiered in 1999 and starred Martin Sheen as President Jed Bartlet, won four consecutive Emmy Awards for Outstanding Drama Series.
This massive Allied invasion on June 6, 1944, saw troops storm the beaches of Normandy, France, in the largest seaborne invasion in history.
This Japanese city was struck by the second atomic bomb dropped by the United States on August 9, 1945, three days after Hiroshima.
This Nazi concentration and extermination camp in occupied Poland was the largest of its kind and the site of over one million deaths, mostly Jewish prisoners.
This classic cocktail, made with tequila, lime juice, and triple sec, is traditionally served in a glass with a salted rim.
This American whiskey, distilled from a grain mash of at least 51% corn and aged in charred new oak barrels, is primarily produced in Kentucky.
This bright red Italian bitter liqueur is a key ingredient in the classic Negroni, combined with gin and sweet vermouth.
This small yellow electric Pokémon is the mascot of the Pokémon franchise and one of the most recognizable characters in gaming history.
This Nintendo console, released in 2017, was revolutionary for its hybrid design — functioning both as a traditional home TV console and a handheld portable device.

This 1985 Nintendo platformer, in which a plumber runs through eight worlds to rescue a princess from a giant turtle-like king, is credited with reviving the home video game industry after the 1983 market crash.
This Greek goddess of love and beauty, associated with the Roman goddess Venus, was said to have been born from the sea foam.
This son of the craftsman Daedalus flew too close to the sun on wings made of feathers and wax, causing them to melt and sending him plunging into the sea.
This greatest warrior of Troy, son of King Priam, was eventually slain in single combat by the Greek hero Achilles during the Trojan War.
This country legend, known as 'The Man in Black', recorded over a thousand songs and is famous for hits like 'Ring of Fire' and 'Folsom Prison Blues'.
This singer-songwriter debuted with a country album at age 16 and later became one of the best-selling artists of all time after crossing over into pop, known for albums like '1989' and 'Fearless'.
This Texas singer-songwriter, known for his long braids and outlaw country style, recorded the landmark album 'Stardust' and is famous for songs like 'On the Road Again' and 'Always on My Mind'.
This Scandinavian country, home to dramatic fjords and the cities of Oslo and Bergen, shares the Scandinavian Peninsula with Sweden.
This river, the longest in Europe at over 2,300 miles, flows through central Russia and empties into the Caspian Sea.
This tiny mountain principality nestled between France and Spain in the Pyrenees is one of the smallest nations in Europe, known for its ski resorts and duty-free shopping.
This 1989 Disney animated film features a mermaid princess named Ariel who longs to live on land and falls in love with a human prince.
This 1991 Disney animated film — the first animated feature nominated for the Academy Award for Best Picture — features a young woman named Belle who falls in love with a cursed prince.
This 1940 Disney animated film pairs classical music by composers like Beethoven and Bach with animated sequences, including a famous segment featuring Mickey Mouse as a sorcerer's apprentice.
This element, with the symbol H and atomic number 1, is the lightest and most abundant element in the universe.
This chart, first published by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869, organizes all known chemical elements by their atomic number and groups them by recurring chemical properties.
This chemical reaction, in which a substance rapidly combines with oxygen and releases heat and light, is the scientific basis for fire.
This Confederate general, commander of the Army of Northern Virginia, surrendered to Ulysses S. Grant at Appomattox Court House on April 9, 1865, effectively ending the Civil War.
This 1863 executive order, issued by President Abraham Lincoln, declared all enslaved people in Confederate states to be legally free.
This three-day battle, fought in Pennsylvania in July 1863, resulted in over 50,000 casualties and is considered the turning point of the Civil War, repelling Lee's final invasion of the North.
This British rock band, featuring Mick Jagger and Keith Richards, has been billed as 'The Greatest Rock and Roll Band in the World' and recorded classics like 'Paint It Black' and 'Gimme Shelter'.
This guitarist, known for songs like 'Purple Haze' and 'Little Wing' and his flamboyant stage presence, is widely considered the greatest electric guitarist in rock history.
This 1971 Led Zeppelin track, often cited as the greatest rock song ever recorded, opens with a delicate acoustic guitar intro and builds to a thunderous electric finale.
This massive fish, the largest species of fish in the world, is a docile filter-feeder that subsists primarily on plankton despite growing up to 40 feet long.
This apex ocean predator — the world's largest predatory fish — is recognized by its rows of serrated triangular teeth, grey dorsal coloring, and pale white underbelly.
This deep-sea fish uses a bioluminescent lure dangling from its forehead to attract prey in the pitch-black depths of the ocean.
This Saiyan warrior is the main protagonist of Dragon Ball Z, known for his spiky black hair, orange gi, and his ability to transform into a Super Saiyan.
This powerful technique, used by Goku and Vegeta, requires two fighters of equal power to mirror each other's movements perfectly and fuse into a single warrior.
This ancient Namekian dragon, summoned by collecting all seven Namekian Dragon Balls, is notably larger and more powerful than his Earth counterpart Shenron.
This 1818 Mary Shelley novel follows a scientist named Victor who stitches together a living creature from body parts and is widely considered the first science fiction novel.
This Herman Melville novel, published in 1851, follows the obsessed Captain Ahab and his crew as they sail the world's oceans hunting a massive white sperm whale.
This Jane Austen novel, published in 1813, follows the witty and independent Elizabeth Bennet as she navigates society, family, and her complicated feelings toward the proud Mr. Darcy.
This NBA franchise, based in Los Angeles, holds the record for the most championships with 17 titles.
This Chicago Bulls shooting guard, who won six NBA championships and five MVP awards, is widely considered the greatest basketball player of all time.
This 6'9" point guard for the Los Angeles Lakers, nicknamed 'Magic', revolutionized his position with his extraordinary passing ability and basketball IQ alongside Kareem Abdul-Jabbar.
This massive oval amphitheater in Rome, completed around 80 AD, could hold up to 80,000 spectators and hosted gladiatorial contests and public spectacles.
This Roman general and statesman, who conquered Gaul and famously crossed the Rubicon, was assassinated by a group of senators on the Ides of March in 44 BC.
This first Roman emperor, who ruled from 27 BC to 14 AD, transformed Rome from a republic into an empire after defeating rivals Mark Antony and Cleopatra at the Battle of Actium.
This 1984 Ivan Reitman comedy follows three parapsychology professors who start a ghost-catching business in New York City, armed with proton packs.
This 1985 Robert Zemeckis film follows teenager Marty McFly, who is accidentally sent back in time to 1955 in a DeLorean time machine built by his eccentric scientist friend.
This 1982 John Carpenter film follows a group of Antarctic researchers who discover a terrifying shape-shifting alien organism capable of perfectly imitating any living creature.
This North African country is home to the Nile River delta, the Sahara Desert, and the ancient pyramids of Giza.
This country at the southern tip of Africa has three capital cities — Pretoria, Cape Town, and Bloemfontein — and is home to the largest urban area on the continent.
This small landlocked kingdom, entirely surrounded by South Africa, is the highest country in the world by average elevation and is home to the Basotho people.
This English scientist formulated the three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation, reportedly inspired by watching an apple fall.
This famous equation, derived by Albert Einstein, expresses that energy equals mass multiplied by the speed of light squared.

This phenomenon, predicted by Einstein's general theory of relativity and first directly detected by LIGO in 2015, consists of ripples in the fabric of spacetime caused by massive accelerating objects.
This Shakespeare tragedy follows a Danish prince who must avenge his father's murder after his uncle seizes the throne and marries his mother.
This Shakespeare tragedy features the Moorish general Othello, his devoted wife Desdemona, and the manipulative villain Iago who destroys them both with jealousy.
This Shakespeare comedy, set in the magical Forest of Arden, features the resourceful heroine Rosalind who disguises herself as a young man named Ganymede.
In baseball, this scoring play occurs when a batter hits the ball and successfully advances around all four bases in a single at-bat.
This legendary New York Yankees slugger set a single-season record of 60 home runs in 1927 and is widely considered the greatest baseball player of all time.
This Brooklyn and Los Angeles Dodgers left-handed pitcher threw four no-hitters in his career, including a perfect game in 1965, before retiring at age 30 due to severe arthritis in his pitching arm.
This Italian dessert, whose name means 'pick me up', is made with layers of espresso-soaked ladyfingers and a rich mascarpone cream.
This long French pastry, made from choux dough and typically filled with cream and glazed with chocolate, takes its name from the French word for 'lightning'.
This classic French upside-down tart, made with caramelized apples baked on a pastry base then inverted when served, was said to have been invented accidentally by the Tatin sisters in the 1880s.
This one-eyed Norse god, the king of the Aesir and god of wisdom, rides an eight-legged horse named Sleipnir and sacrificed his eye to drink from the well of knowledge.
This enormous sea serpent of Norse mythology, offspring of Loki, encircles the entire world and bites its own tail, giving it the nickname 'the World Serpent'.
This Norse goddess of love, fertility, and war leads the Valkyries and receives half of those slain in battle into her hall, Sessrúmnir, in the realm of Fólkvangr.
This tiny city-state on the southern tip of the Malay Peninsula, once a British colony, is now one of the world's leading financial centers and busiest ports.
This East Asian island nation, composed of over 6,800 islands, is the world's most populous island nation and is home to cities like Tokyo, Osaka, and Kyoto.
This landlocked nation between Russia and China is the world's most sparsely populated independent country, known for its vast steppes, the Gobi Desert, and its nomadic herding traditions.
This CBS sitcom, which premiered in 2007, follows the socially awkward physicist Sheldon Cooper and his group of nerdy scientist friends navigating relationships and pop culture.
This NBC mockumentary workplace comedy, set at a paper company in Scranton, Pennsylvania, was adapted from a British series and made Steve Carell a household name.

This FX/FXX sitcom, set in a grimy Philadelphia dive bar, follows a group of self-absorbed friends who own Paddy's Pub and has aired for over 15 seasons, making it the longest-running live-action comedy in American TV history.
This small, green Jedi Master, who trained both Obi-Wan Kenobi and Luke Skywalker, is famous for speaking in an inverted syntax.
This 1980 Star Wars sequel, widely considered the best film in the franchise, reveals a shocking family secret between Luke Skywalker and the villain Darth Vader.

valid: star wars the empire strikes back

questions.question: This Mandalorian-armored bounty hunter appeared in the original trilogy as the man hired by Darth Vader to track down and capture Han Solo.
This muscular organ, located in the chest, pumps blood continuously throughout the body via an electrical system that keeps it beating around 100,000 times per day.
This largest organ of the human body serves as a barrier against infection, regulates body temperature, and is constantly regenerating its outer layers.
This small, finger-shaped pouch attached to the large intestine has no known digestive function in modern humans and can become dangerously inflamed, requiring emergency surgical removal.
This 1776 document, written primarily by Thomas Jefferson, formally declared the thirteen American colonies independent from British rule.
This 1773 act of colonial protest, in which demonstrators disguised as Native Americans dumped an entire shipment of tea into a harbor, helped ignite the Revolutionary War.
This Prussian military officer hired by George Washington spent the brutal winter of 1777–1778 drilling and professionalizing the ragged Continental Army at Valley Forge.
This British rock band, formed in 1968 and featuring guitarist Jimmy Page and vocalist Robert Plant, is known for epic songs like 'Whole Lotta Love' and 'Kashmir'.
This flamboyant British musician, known for theatrical stage personas like 'Ziggy Stardust', recorded landmark albums such as 'Heroes' and 'Let's Dance' across several decades.

This Canadian rock trio — featuring guitarist Alex Lifeson, bassist and vocalist Geddy Lee, and drummer Neil Peart — is celebrated for technically complex, progressive compositions spanning over four decades.
This Vietnamese noodle soup, made with a rich, slow-simmered broth, rice noodles, herbs, and thinly sliced meat, is considered the national dish of Vietnam.
This Spanish dish of saffron-seasoned rice cooked with seafood, chicken, or vegetables in a wide, shallow pan originated in the Valencia region.
This Georgian dish consists of bread baked in the shape of a boat, filled with molten cheese, and finished with a raw egg and a pat of butter melted into the center.
This 2003 Pixar film follows a clownfish named Marlin who searches the entire ocean for his missing son, with help from a forgetful fish named Dory.
This 1995 Pixar film — the studio's first feature-length movie — introduced audiences to cowboy toy Woody and space ranger Buzz Lightyear competing for the affection of their owner Andy.

This 2001 Hayao Miyazaki film follows a young girl named Chihiro who wanders into a spirit world and must work at a magical bathhouse run by a witch to save her parents, who have been transformed into pigs.
This invention, significantly improved by James Watt in the 1760s, powered factories, mills, and locomotives and is considered the defining engine of the Industrial Revolution.
This northern English city, nicknamed 'Cottonopolis', became the world's first industrial city and the global center of cotton textile production in the 19th century.
This early 19th-century movement saw British textile workers smash industrial machinery in protest against automation threatening their livelihoods, named after a mythical working-class folk hero.
This 1978 John Carpenter film features a masked killer named Michael Myers who stalks babysitters on Halloween night in the fictional town of Haddonfield, Illinois.
This 1980 Stanley Kubrick film, based on a Stephen King novel, follows writer Jack Torrance as he descends into murderous madness while caretaking an isolated mountain hotel with his family.
This 1973 William Friedkin film depicts the demonic possession of a young girl in Washington D.C. and is widely considered one of the scariest and most influential horror films ever made.
This Swiss tennis player, who retired in 2022 after winning 20 Grand Slam titles, is considered one of the greatest players in the history of the sport.
This annual Grand Slam tournament, held at the All England Club in London, is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and is played on grass courts.
This Spanish player, nicknamed 'The King of Clay', won the French Open a record 14 times and accumulated 22 Grand Slam titles in total before retiring in 2024.
This Irish actor played James Bond in seven films from 1995 to 2002, including 'GoldenEye' and 'Die Another Day'.
This 2012 Sam Mendes-directed Bond film starring Daniel Craig is the highest-grossing film in the franchise and features the villain Silva threatening M and MI6.
This fictional British intelligence agency, where James Bond holds a 00 status and license to kill, is headquartered at Vauxhall Cross in London.
This ancient Greek city-state was famous for its military culture, requiring boys to leave home and begin military training at age seven.
This Greek philosopher, tutor of Alexander the Great, wrote extensively on topics ranging from biology and physics to politics and ethics, founding the Lyceum school in Athens.
This Greek mathematician from Alexandria, who lived around 300 BC, wrote 'Elements' — a foundational geometry text that remained the standard curriculum in schools for over two thousand years.
This fast food chain, whose mascot is a red-headed clown, is the world's largest fast food corporation by number of locations with over 40,000 restaurants worldwide.
This fast food chain, known for its square hamburger patties, was founded by Dave Thomas in Columbus, Ohio in 1969 and became famous for its 'Where's the beef?' ad campaign.

This fast food chain, headquartered in Louisville, Kentucky and founded by Harland Sanders, specializes in fried chicken and operates in over 150 countries, making it one of the most globally widespread restaurant chains.
This Nintendo plumber is the company's mascot and stars in dozens of platform games set in the Mushroom Kingdom.
This open-world survival game, created by Markus Persson, lets players build and explore worlds made of blocks and features enemies called Creepers.
This Nintendo franchise stars a hero named Link who must rescue Princess Zelda and defeat the villain Ganon across a series of adventure games.
This organ pumps blood throughout the entire body and beats around 100,000 times per day.
This bone in the upper leg is the longest and strongest bone in the human body.
This small gland at the base of the brain is often called the 'master gland' because it controls other hormone-producing glands throughout the body.
This British rock band, fronted by Mick Jagger, released iconic songs like 'Paint It Black' and 'Sympathy for the Devil'.
This legendary guitarist, known for his psychedelic style and iconic Woodstock performance, released 'Purple Haze' and 'All Along the Watchtower'.
This Irish rock band, formed in Dublin in 1976, is known for anthems like 'With or Without You,' 'One,' and 'Where the Streets Have No Name'.
This professional American football league, consisting of 32 teams, hosts the Super Bowl each year as its championship game.
This quarterback won 7 Super Bowl championships over his career and is widely regarded as the greatest to ever play the position.
This San Francisco 49ers quarterback won four Super Bowls in the 1980s and never threw an interception in Super Bowl play.
This Japanese dish consists of vinegared rice topped or wrapped with raw fish, vegetables, or other ingredients.
This soft Indian flatbread is traditionally cooked in a clay tandoor oven and commonly served alongside curries.
This Georgian dish of cheese-filled bread — often boat-shaped and topped with a cracked egg and pat of butter — is considered the national comfort food of the country of Georgia.
This king of the Greek gods rules from Mount Olympus and is associated with lightning bolts and thunder.
This Greek hero used a mirrored shield to slay the snake-haired Gorgon Medusa without looking at her directly.
This master craftsman of Greek mythology built wings of feathers and wax for himself and his son, who later flew too close to the sun.
This 1937 Disney animated film — the studio's very first feature-length movie — tells the tale of a princess who hides with seven miners after fleeing a jealous queen.

valid: snow white and the seven dwarfs

questions.question: This Disney princess, locked in a tower by a witch, has 70 feet of magical golden hair with healing powers and is rescued by a charming thief named Flynn Rider.
This 1994 Disney animated film, set in 15th-century Peru, features a self-absorbed emperor named Kuzco who is transformed into a llama by his scheming advisor Yzma.
This colorless gas makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere and is a key component of proteins and DNA.
This type of chemical bond forms when two atoms share electrons, allowing both atoms to achieve a stable electron configuration — as seen in water (H₂O) and carbon dioxide (CO₂).
This metabolic process, carried out by yeast and bacteria, converts sugars into alcohol and carbon dioxide and is essential to brewing beer, making wine, and baking bread.
This South American city, the highest capital city in the world at about 11,800 feet above sea level, sits on a high plateau in the Andes and is the seat of government for Bolivia.
Many people assume Istanbul is the capital of Turkey, but this inland city on the Anatolian plateau actually holds that distinction.
This city, perched over 11,900 feet above sea level in the Andes, serves as the seat of government and de facto capital of Bolivia.
This rapper, born Shawn Carter in Brooklyn, co-founded Roc-A-Fella Records and is one of the best-selling music artists of all time.
This N.W.A. founding member launched a legendary solo career with the 1992 album 'The Chronic' and later co-founded Death Row Records.
This Compton rapper's 2015 album 'To Pimp a Butterfly,' blending jazz, funk, and spoken word, is widely considered one of the greatest hip hop albums ever made.
This great network of roads, aqueducts, and infrastructure built by the Romans was so enduring that many routes are still in use today, summed up by the phrase 'all roads lead to' this city.

This ancient Roman road, constructed beginning in 312 BC and still partially intact today, was the primary route from Rome south to Brindisi and is considered one of the greatest roads of the ancient world.
This ancient Roman road, stretching over 350 miles from Rome to the port city of Brindisi, was the main route to Greece and the eastern Mediterranean.
This slow-moving, plant-eating sea creature — related to the elephant on land — is also called a sea cow and is frequently injured by boat propellers in shallow coastal waters.
This large predatory fish is considered the fastest in the ocean, capable of swimming over 68 miles per hour, and is named for the large fin rising from its back.
This cephalopod, the giant version of which has the largest eyes of any animal on Earth at up to 10 inches in diameter, was long considered a mythical sea monster called the Kraken.
This 1975 Steven Spielberg thriller features a great white shark terrorizing a New England beach town and is credited with creating the summer blockbuster concept.
In this 1980 Stanley Kubrick horror film, a writer played by Jack Nicholson slowly goes mad while serving as caretaker of the isolated Overlook Hotel.
This 1973 horror film, based on a novel by William Peter Blatty, follows a family whose young daughter Regan becomes possessed by a demonic entity.
This classic French pastry is made from choux dough piped into an oblong shape, filled with cream, and topped with chocolate glaze.
This popular Italian dessert is made with espresso-soaked ladyfingers layered with a rich mascarpone cream and dusted with cocoa powder.
This French confection, made from almond flour, egg whites, and sugar, is sandwiched together with ganache, buttercream, or jam filling and comes in a rainbow of colors.
This concrete barrier, constructed in 1961, physically divided the city of Berlin between East and West for 28 years until it fell in 1989.
This 1962 political standoff between the U.S. and Soviet Union, triggered by the placement of nuclear missiles in Cuba, is considered the closest the Cold War came to nuclear conflict.
This 1947 U.S. foreign policy, named after a sitting president, pledged American support to nations threatened by Soviet-backed communist expansion, starting with Greece and Turkey.
This NBA player, nicknamed 'King James,' broke the league's all-time scoring record in 2023 and has won four NBA championships across three different teams.
This NBA franchise, based in Boston and playing in green uniforms, holds the record for the most NBA championships won by any team in history.
This LSU guard, nicknamed 'Pistol Pete,' set an NCAA record by averaging 44.2 points per game in a single season and dazzled fans with his behind-the-back passes and flashy dribbling.
This Frenchman painted 'Water Lilies', a series of roughly 250 oil paintings depicting his flower garden at Giverny, and is considered the father of Impressionism.

This Mexican painter, known for her self-portraits and works inspired by pre-Columbian art, was married to muralist Diego Rivera and painted 'The Two Fridas' while recovering from a devastating bus accident.

This Austrian symbolist painter, known for his gilded, sensuous style, created 'The Kiss' and 'Portrait of Adele Bloch-Bauer I', which was famously looted by the Nazis and later returned to the family.
This Western European country is home to the Eiffel Tower, the Louvre museum, and the Palace of Versailles.

This large island, the third-largest in the Mediterranean Sea, is divided between Greece, Turkey, and a self-declared Turkish republic in the north, and is known in mythology as the birthplace of Aphrodite.
This tiny republic perched atop Mount Titano is completely surrounded by Italy and is considered the world's oldest surviving sovereign state, founded in 301 AD.
This long-running animated sitcom, set in the fictional town of Springfield, follows the dysfunctional family of Homer, Marge, Bart, Lisa, and Maggie.
This animated adult comedy series follows four foul-mouthed boys — Stan, Kyle, Cartman, and Kenny — in the small Colorado mountain town of the show's title.
This animated Fox sitcom follows the Belcher family — Bob, Linda, and their three kids Tina, Gene, and Louise — who run a struggling burger restaurant.
This cocktail made with vodka and tomato juice is seasoned with hot sauce and Worcestershire sauce and is a popular brunch drink often garnished with celery.
This classic cocktail from Cuba is made with white rum, fresh lime juice, sugar, and muddled mint leaves, served over crushed ice.
This iconic cocktail, made with gin and dry vermouth and garnished with an olive or a lemon twist, is associated with sophistication and with a certain fictional British spy.
This immense world-tree in Norse mythology connects the nine realms of existence, including Asgard above and Niflheim below, and is constantly gnawed at by the dragon Níðhöggr.
This shape-shifting trickster god of Norse mythology is the cunning son of a frost giant and adoptive brother of Thor, and is the father of both the wolf Fenrir and the world serpent.
This giant serpent of Norse mythology encircles the entire world at the bottom of the ocean, biting its own tail, and is destined to fight and kill Thor during Ragnarök.
This Marvel superhero, a Wakandan king played by the late Chadwick Boseman in the MCU, wears a suit made of vibranium and was the first Black superhero to headline a major Hollywood blockbuster.
This 2018 MCU film follows the villain Thanos on his quest to collect all six Infinity Stones and wipe out half of all life in the universe.
This MCU hero, played by Chadwick Boseman, is the king and protector of the fictional African nation of Wakanda, powered by the rare metal vibranium.
This type of cell division produces four daughter cells each with half the number of chromosomes of the parent cell and is the basis for sexual reproduction in plants and animals.
This English naturalist published 'On the Origin of Species' in 1859, introducing the world to the theory of evolution by natural selection.
This process, by which a single fertilized egg divides and develops into a complete multicellular organism with distinct tissues and organs, is studied in the field of developmental biology.
This document, adopted on July 4, 1776, announced that the thirteen American colonies were separating from British rule and establishing a new nation.
This Virginia planter and general commanded the Continental Army throughout the Revolutionary War and later became the first President of the United States.

This 1775 battle, fought on a hill near Boston, was one of the first major engagements of the Revolution, where colonial militiamen inflicted heavy casualties on British forces despite ultimately retreating.
This country musician, known as 'The Man in Black,' released classics like 'Ring of Fire,' 'Folsom Prison Blues,' and later a celebrated cover of Nine Inch Nails' 'Hurt.'
This Tennessee country superstar is known for heartfelt hits like 'Jolene' and 'I Will Always Love You' and is also a prolific philanthropist who funded COVID-19 vaccine research.
This Oklahoma country singer became one of the best-selling music artists of all time with hits like 'Friends in Low Places,' 'The Dance,' and 'Unanswered Prayers.'
This East Asian country, the world's most populous nation, is home to the Great Wall, the Forbidden City, and the Yangtze River.
This island nation south of India, known for its tea plantations and ancient Buddhist temples, was formerly called Ceylon and has Colombo as its commercial capital.

This landlocked country in Central Asia, entirely surrounded by other landlocked countries, making it one of only two such doubly landlocked nations in the world, was once the heart of the Silk Road and today its capital is Tashkent.
This is Earth's only natural satellite and the fifth largest moon in the solar system, visible from Earth every night.
This space telescope, launched in 1990 and orbiting Earth at about 340 miles up, has captured iconic images of distant galaxies, nebulae, and the deep universe.
This vast, spherical cloud of icy bodies surrounding the solar system at a distance of up to a light-year from the sun is believed to be the source of long-period comets.
This beloved NBC sitcom, set primarily in New York City, follows six friends — Ross, Rachel, Monica, Chandler, Joey, and Phoebe — through their daily lives over 10 seasons.
This mockumentary-style NBC sitcom, set at the Scranton branch of the Dunder Mifflin Paper Company, features the clueless but loveable boss Michael Scott.
This critically acclaimed Fox sitcom about the dysfunctional Bluth family, led by the long-suffering son Michael, was cancelled after three seasons then later revived on Netflix.
This fundamental force of nature keeps planets in orbit around stars, moons in orbit around planets, and objects attached to the surface of Earth.
This English mathematician and physicist formulated the three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation, and independently co-invented calculus.

This thought experiment, proposed by Erwin Schrödinger in 1935 to illustrate a paradox of quantum superposition, involves a hypothetical cat that is simultaneously alive and dead until it is observed.
This mountain on the border of Nepal and China is the highest point on Earth, standing at 29,032 feet above sea level.
This vast hot desert, covering most of North Africa, is the largest hot desert in the world at approximately 3.5 million square miles.
This mountain range in South America, running about 4,300 miles along the western edge of the continent, is the longest continental mountain range in the world.

This Nintendo fighting game franchise, in which characters from across gaming history battle each other, is one of the best-selling series on Nintendo platforms and features characters like Kirby, Fox McCloud, and Link.
This Nintendo racing franchise lets players choose from a cast of beloved characters and race across creative themed tracks, using items like shells and banana peels to gain an edge.
This Nintendo franchise stars a space bounty hunter named Samus Aran who explores alien planets and battles Space Pirates and their fearsome leader Ridley.
This brass instrument, most associated with jazz legend Louis Armstrong, features a flared bell and three valves and often leads the melody in jazz and blues ensembles.
This alto saxophonist, nicknamed 'Bird,' was a pioneering figure of the fast and complex bebop style of jazz and recorded groundbreaking sessions in the 1940s.
This Mississippi Delta blues guitarist, who recorded in the 1930s and died at 27, is the subject of the legend that he sold his soul at a crossroads in exchange for extraordinary guitar skills.
This tall, four-sided stone monument, typically topped with a pyramid-shaped cap, was built by the ancient Egyptians and placed in pairs at temple entrances to honor gods and pharaohs.

This last active ruler of the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt is known for her relationships with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony and her political genius in trying to preserve Egyptian independence from Rome.
This ancient Egyptian deity, depicted with the head of a jackal and the body of a man, presided over mummification and guided souls through the underworld.
